content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660         322-5901         322-6441         322-6444


May 3, 1983


Thomas J. Harron
Assistant City Attorney
City of Chula Vista
276 Fourth Avenue
Chula Vista, CA  91010

Re: Your Request for Written
    Advice on Behalf of David
    Malcolm, Councilmember
    Our File No. A-83-100

Dear Mr. Harron:

     Your letter contains a very thorough discussion of the facts
which you have been kind enough to supplement with maps,
photographs, and aerial photographs. I incorporate your
statement of the facts and the question presented, which are
quoted below:

     The facts are that Councilman David Malcolm owns
     approximately 4 acres of undeveloped real property in the
     vicinity of Palomas and Bay Boulevard next to the Chula
     Vista Bayfront. The property is approximately 1/2 mile
     south of "L" Street which constitutes the southern boundary
     of Chula Vista's Redevelopment Area. The Redevelopment
     Agency will be considering a number of proposals for
     development in the Bayfront area. These plans focus on the
     area around "F" Street and extend as far south as "G" Street
     which is approximately 1-1/2 miles north of Councilman
     Malcolm's property. The San Diego Gas & Electric power
     plant is located to the north of Malcolm's property and
     serves as a barrier between the property and the southern
     boundary of the Redevelopment Area. Just south of Councilman
     Malcolm's property is the property owned and operated by
     Western Salt. Councilman Malcolm's property is zoned "I",
     heavy industrial. The Malcolm property is located near the
     southern end of the Bayfront where the water is very
     shallow. The water next to Malcolm's property is actually
     not part of the Bayfront but rather consists of man-made

Thomas J. Harron
May 3, 1983
Page 2

dikes used by Western Salt. This has a very limiting effect
on any uses which the Bayfront location might offer to the
property.

While the SDG&E power plant forms a barrier between
Malcolm's property and the southern end of the Redevelopment
area, there is also another substantial industrial use
between the Malcolm property and the area under
consideration. That is the property owned by Rohr
Industries and it is focused around "H" Street. The plans
which will be under consideration involve the development of
the Bayfront around "F" Street and as far south as "G"
Street. The plan provides for multiple uses in that area.
They include tourist, commercial, residential and light
industrial. Since the area of the Bayfront under
consideration is presently a Conglomeration of vacant marsh
land, some farm land and undeveloped flat land, any
development proposal which the Redevelopment Agency should
select should serve to greatly improve this area.

The question is whether under Government Code Section 87103
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions made by the
Redevelopment Agency on the Bayfront Redevelopment Plan for
the "F" to "G" Street area will have a material financial
effect which is distinguishable from its effect on the
public generally on Malcolm's property.

You have also stated that, at my suggestion, you contacted
the County Assessor's Office to determine whether any effect on
the value of Mr. Malcolm's property was foreseeable. Mr. Ron
Anderson of that office told you that whenever you have
development of one portion of a scarce resource, i.e., bayfront
property, it leaves less of that resource to be developed and
under the general rules of supply and demand the value of the
remaining scarce resource is enhanced. He further stated that
the real question is not whether the remaining property would be
enhanced, but to what degree. He felt that it would be too
speculative to try to address that question.

In addition, you have made an analysis of the situation
which includes the following statements:

Since Malcolm's property is vacant but is bordered by heavy
industrial use, it is reasonable to conclude that his
property will have a related use. It is difficult to say
what the effect of the plan under consideration could have

Thomas J. Harron
May 3, 1983
Page 3


on that use and any attempt to do so would be speculative.
There is no identifiable "peculiar" impact on his property.
Also there is no immediate effect on his property any
different from property throughout the City.

You think the most significant factor in isolating the
Malcolm property from any potential effect is the barrier which
the SDG&E power plant places between him and the Bayfront. The
plant is a heavy industrial use and it dominates the area.

It is hard to believe with Malcolm's location south of that
plant, that he can in some way tie in the development of his
property with the development which occurs to the north.

We agree with your conclusion that Mr. Malcolm's property
will not be affected materially/ by the currently pending
decision. While immediate proximity is not an absolute
prerequisite for a decision to have a material financial effect,
it is an important factor to be taken into consideration.2/
Here, the subject property is 1-1/2 miles from the nearest
portion of the project in question. The project involves
improvement of Chula Vista's bayfront; however, Mr. Malcolm's
property does not front on the Bay and is separated from the
project by substantial intervening developments which
significantly buffer any effects on his property.

Apparently, the only identifiable effect resulting from the
development of the land within the proposed project would be
from the reduction of a scarce resource. This is both difficult

1/ Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 require an
official to disqualify nimself if a decision will materially
affect a financial interest. A financial interest in a decision
is present if an official, such as Mr. Malcolm, holds an
interest in real property worth more than $1,000 (he does) which
will reasonably and foreseeably be materially affected by the
decision.

2/ For example, if a councilmember owned the only large
motel in town and the council was considering a land use matter
which would result in the construction of a new
Disneyland/Disneyworld type facility 4 miles away from his
hotel, the distance would not rule out the possibility that
there would be a material financial effect resulting from the
decision.

Thomas J. Harron
May 3, 1983
Page 4


to quantify and to project in terms of time. It is equally true
that any other development of the project property (perhaps
entirely by private interests) would have the same impact.
Therefore, it is difficult to say that any increase in the value
of Mr. Malcolm's property is directly attributable to the
project decision. Furthermore, we have previously said that
where the foreseeable effect (if any) of a decision will only
occur (if at all) at a remote time, the effect is not reasonably
foreseeable. See Government Code Section 87103.

     Should you have further questions regarding this matter, I
may be reached at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Robert E. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:plh

The City of Chula Vista
Office of the City Attorney    (619) 691-5037

March 29, 1983

Mr. Robert E. Leidigh
Counsel Legal Division
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA.  95804

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

This letter is a follow-up to our telephone conversation regarding
the question of whether one of our City Councilmen may have a
conflict of interest. By this letter, I would request that the
Fair Political Practices Commission author an opinion in this
matter.

The facts are that Councilman David Malcolm owns approximately 4
acres of undeveloped real property in the vicinity of Palomar and
Bay Boulevard next to the Chula Vista Bayfront. The property is
1100 feet from the mean high tide line. This property is approximately 1/2 mile south of "I" which constitutes the southern
boundary of Chula Vista's Redevelopment Area. The Redevelopment
Agency will be considering a number of proposals for development
in the Bayfront area. These plans focus on the area around "F"
Street and extend as far south as "G" Street which is approximately 1-1/2 miles north of Councilman Malcolm's property. The
San Diego Gas & Electric power plant is located to the north of
Malcolm's property and serves as a barrier between the property
and the southern boundary of the Redevelopment Area. Just south
of Councilman Malcolm's property is the property owned and
operated by Western Salt. Councilman Malcolm's property is zoned
"I", heavy industrial. The Malcolm property is located near the
southern end of the Bayfront where the water is very shallow. The
water next to Malcolm's property is actually not part of the
Bayfront but rather consists of man-made dikes used by Western
Salt. This has a very limiting effect on any uses which the
Bayfront location might offer to the property.

While the SDG&E power plant forms a barrier between Malcolm's
property and the southern end of the Redevelopment area, there is
also another substantial industrial use between the Malcolm
property and the area under consideration. That is the property
owned by Rohr Industries and it is focused around "H" Street.

276 Fourth Avenue   Chula Vista, California 92010

Mr. Robert E. Leidigh
March 29, 1983
Page Two


The plans which will be under consideration involve the
development of the Bayfront around "F" Street and as far south as
"G" Street. The plan provides for multiple uses in that area.
They include tourist, commercial, residential and light
industrial. Since the area of the Bayfront under consideration is
presently a conglomeration of vacant marsh land, some farm land
and undeveloped flat land, any development proposal which the
Redevelopment Agency should select should serve to greatly improve
this area.

The question is whether under Government Code Section 87103 it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decisions made by the Redevelopment Agency on the Bayfront Redevelopment Plan for the "F" to "G"
Street area will have a material financial effect which is
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally on
Malcolm's property. As I previously told you, it was my opinion
that it would not, but I feel uncomfortable with being put in the
role of a property appraiser trying to speculate what effect the
development of the "F" to "G" Street area could have on properties
to the south. At your suggestion, I made a call to the County
Assessor's office to determine how they would view such a situation. I spoke to Ron Anderson of that office and he told me that
whenever you have development of one portion of a scare resource,
i.e., bayfront property, it leaves less of that resource to be
developed and under the general rules of supply and demand the
value of the remaining scare resource is enhanced. He further
stated that the real question is not whether the remaining property would be enhanced, but to what degree. He felt that it
would be too speculative to try to address that question.

I researched the Morrow decision at 70 C.A.3d 817, and I have also
read the FPPC's opinions on conflict of interest dealing with
property ownership near a "core area". From that research, I have
tried to glean some of the factors which are considered important
in determining whether a conflict of interest exists. They seem
to include:

     1.  Proximity to the "core area";

     2.  The "segment of the public" in the same position;

     3.  The speculative versus certain effect of the plan on the
         property;

     4.  Any "peculiar" impact on the official's property; and

     5.  The immediacy of the effect on the property.

Mr. Robert E. Leidigh
March 29, 1983
Page Three


In applying these factors to the Malcolm property, I noticed that
all of the previous opinions dealt with property that was
contiguous to the "core area". Malcolm's property is located at a
considerable distance from the area under consideration,
approximately 1-1/2 miles from the "core area" and 1/2 mile from
the Redevelopment Area in general.

In considering the "segment of the public" in the same position,
there are really two ways to look at it. If we consider only
Bayfront property owners, there are not that many people in the
same position. However, if we consider property owners in general
within a 1-1/2 mile area or even a 1/2 mile area, then the numbers
are substantial. I think that the fact that Malcolm's property is
surrounded by man-made dikes and is 1100 feet from the mean high
tide line rules out normal Bayfront type uses of his property and
he should be classified in the larger category of property owners
in general.

Since Malcolm's property is vacant but is bordered by heavy
industrial use, it is reasonable to conclude that his property
will have a related use. It is difficult to say what the effect
of the plan under consideration could have on that use and any
attempt to do so would be speculative. There is no identifiable
"peculiar" impact on his property. Also there is no immediate
effect on his property any different from property throughout the
City.

I think the most significant factor in isolating the Malcolm
property from any potential effect is the barrier which the SDG&E
power plant places between him and the Bayfront. The plant is a
heavy industrial use and it dominates the area. It is hard to
believe with Malcolm's location south of that plant, that he can
in some way tie in the development of his property with the
development which occurs to the north.

Enclosed are some pictures of the area and a map of the Bayfront
to enable you to get a feel for the facts of this case. If there
is any further information which I could forward to you, please
contact me at (619) 691-5037. Thank you for your cooperation in
this matter.

Yours very truly,

Thomas J. Harron
Assistant City Attorney

Enc.

PHOTOGRAPH 3

NORTH

BAY

BLVD.

CHULA
VISTA

SAN DIEGO

[illegible]

Malcolm's property

sections.question: The question is whether under Government Code Section 87103 it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions made by the Redevelopment Agency on the Bayfront Redevelopment Plan for the "F" to "G" Street area will have a material financial effect which is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally on Malcolm's property.
sections.conclusion: We agree with your conclusion that Mr. Malcolm's property will not be affected materially by the currently pending decision. While immediate proximity is not an absolute prerequisite for a decision to have a material financial effect, it is an important factor to be taken into consideration. Here, the subject property is 1-1/2 miles from the nearest portion of the project in question. The project involves improvement of Chula Vista's bayfront; however, Mr. Malcolm's property does not front on the Bay and is separated from the project by substantial intervening developments which significantly buffer any effects on his property. Apparently, the only identifiable effect resulting from the development of the land within the proposed project would be from the reduction of a scarce resource. This is both difficult to quantify and to project in terms of time. It is equally true that any other development of the project property (perhaps entirely by private interests) would have the same impact. Therefore, it is difficult to say that any increase in the value of Mr. Malcolm's property is directly attributable to the project decision.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Councilman David Malcolm must disqualify himself from voting on a Chula Vista Bayfront Redevelopment Plan because his property ownership (1.5 miles from the project area) would be materially affected by the Redevelopment Agency's decision under Government Code Section 87103.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that Councilman Malcolm's property will not be materially affected by the Redevelopment Agency's decision because the property is 1.5 miles distant, separated by substantial industrial developments, does not front on the Bay, and any potential value increase would be speculative and not directly attributable to the project decision.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (March 29, 1983) and FPPC response letter (May 3, 1983). Both contain identical fact patterns and questions. Analysis extracts from the FPPC response which is the primary advice letter. Footnotes provide statutory references and hypothetical examples clarifying the legal standard.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: The question is whether under Government Code Section 87103 it is reasonably foreseeable that the decisions made by the Redevelopment Agency on the Bayfront Redevelopment Plan for the "F" to "G" Street area will have a material financial effect which is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally on Malcolm's property.

CONCLUSION: We agree with your conclusion that Mr. Malcolm's property will not be affected materially by the currently pending decision. While immediate proximity is not an absolute prerequisite for a decision to have a material financial effect, it is an important factor to be taken into consideration. Here, the subject property is 1-1/2 miles from the nearest portion of the project in question. The project involves improvement of Chula Vista's bayfront; however, Mr. Malcolm's property does not front on the Bay and is separated from the project by substantial intervening developments which significantly buffer any effects on his property. Apparently, the only identifiable effect resulting from the development of the land within the proposed project would be from the reduction of a scarce resource. This is both difficult to quantify and to project in terms of time. It is equally true that any other development of the project property (perhaps entirely by private interests) would have the same impact. Therefore, it is difficult to say that any increase in the value of Mr. Malcolm's property is directly attributable to the project decision.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel Robert E. Leidigh advises that Councilman David Malcolm need not disqualify himself from voting on a Chula Vista Bayfront Redevelopment Plan, as his property 1.5 miles away is unlikely to be materially and foreseeably affected by the decision. The opinion emphasizes that distance, intervening developments, and the speculative nature of any property value impact support the finding of no material financial effect.
